fact: October 22 is exactly 60 days away from the winter solstice in the Northern Hemisphere - that's like counting down two whole months to the shortest day of the year!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, this date marks the peak of spring spider season, when millions of baby spiders balloon through the air on silk threads!
October 22 falls smack in the middle of peak leaf-peeping season in New England, when maple trees turn so bright red they look like they're on fire!
This date sits right in the heart of Libra season, meaning people born on October 22 are ruled by Venus and supposedly obsessed with balance and beauty.
Squirrels go absolutely bonkers around this time of year, burying up to 10,000 nuts per squirrel to prepare for winter - that's like hiding 30 nuts every single day!
October 22 marks the time when Earth's Northern Hemisphere is tilted about 15 degrees away from the sun, making shadows grow noticeably longer each day.
In many cold regions, this date signals the start of 'frost season' when morning grass gets covered in tiny ice crystals that crunch under your feet.
The moon phase on October 22 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, it appears extra bright because the autumn air is crisp and clear.
Monarch butterflies are usually somewhere over Texas or Mexico on October 22, flying thousands of miles south in one of nature's most epic road trips.
This date falls during the peak of mushroom season in many forests, when weird fungi pop up overnight like nature's own alien invasion.
October 22 is roughly when many deciduous trees stop making chlorophyll, which is why their leaves suddenly explode into yellow, orange, and red colors.
